coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the structural, electronic, optical, and gas adsorption properties of transition metal (Cu, Ni, Fe) and alkali metal (Li, Mg) doped anthracene nanotubes (An-NT).Structural optimizations reveal significant changes in bond lengths and angles due to metal doping, with Fe-doped A6-NT showing the most stability (binding energy: 7.63 eV). Electronic analysis indicates that Cu and Li doping reduce the energy gap (Eg), enhancing conductivity in A6-NT (Eg = 0.513 eV) and A7-NT (Eg = 0.601 eV). Conversely, Fe, Ni, and Mg doping lead to wider energy gaps, indicating reduced conductivity. Optical properties reveal significant variations in the absorption spectra, correlating with the density of states (DOS) that shift upon doping, suggesting potential tunability for optoelectronic applications. Gas adsorption studies show enhanced interaction for CH4 and CO2, with Fe-doped A6-NT and A7-NT exhibiting the highest adsorption energies. This positions them as promising candidates for gas sensing and storage applications, showcasing the potential of metal-doped anthracene nanotubes in electronic devices and gas capture technologies.

After doping, “X” is replaced by the respective doping atoms in the modified structures: Cu in A6-NT-6Cu, Fe in A6-NT-6Fe, Ni in A6-NT-6Ni, Li in A6-NT-6Li, and Mg in A6-NT-6 Mg.

head.ce:title: Evaluating the performance of metal-doped anthracene nanotubes for gas sensing applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the structural, electronic, optical, and gas adsorption properties of transition metal (Cu, Ni, Fe) and alkali metal (Li, Mg) doped anthracene nanotubes (An-NT).Structural optimizations reveal significant changes in bond lengths and angles due to metal doping, with Fe-doped A6-NT showing the most stability (binding energy: 7.63 eV). Electronic analysis indicates that Cu and Li doping reduce the energy gap (Eg), enhancing conductivity in A6-NT (Eg = 0.513 eV) and A7-NT (Eg = 0.601 eV). Conversely, Fe, Ni, and Mg doping lead to wider energy gaps, indicating reduced conductivity. Optical properties reveal significant variations in the absorption spectra, correlating with the density of states (DOS) that shift upon doping, suggesting potential tunability for optoelectronic applications. Gas adsorption studies show enhanced interaction for CH4 and CO2, with Fe-doped A6-NT and A7-NT exhibiting the highest adsorption energies. This positions them as promising candidates for gas sensing and storage applications, showcasing the potential of metal-doped anthracene nanotubes in electronic devices and gas capture technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuels, including natural gas and petroleum, remain primary global energy sources [1–4], with natural gas quality influenced by geological conditions [5–7]. CO2, a major impurity in natural gas streams, can reach up to 90 %, reducing calorific value and causing equipment corrosion [7–10]. As energy demand grows, CO2 separation from natural gas is critical for reducing emissions [11–13]. Various technologies, such as absorption, membranes, cryogenic methods, adsorption, and chemical looping combustion, have been developed for efficient CO2 capture [14–18]. Among them, adsorption on porous solids like zeolites [19,20], mesoporous silica [21,22], carbon-based materials [23], and metal–organic frameworks [24] is a promising method. However, these materials are often costly or complex to synthesize, necessitating the search for cost-effective alternatives with high adsorption capacity [25]. Volumetric CO2 adsorption, which considers adsorbent density, is crucial for industrial applications, helping to optimize packed bed efficiency [25–27]. Methane (CH4), the primary component of natural gas, poses risks such as asphyxiation and explosion in confined spaces, making its detection in flammable environments essential [25]. Current methane detection methods include metal oxide sensors [28–30], microbial biosensors [31], and light-emitting diode sensors [32,33], but they require high temperatures and have slow response times [34–37]. Enhancing sensitivity and response times at room temperature is necessary, particularly for pollutants like CO, HCHO, and CH4, which weakly interact with single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) [38–41].

In 1990, Krätschmer and colleagues reported the extraction of C60 and C70 from carbon soot using aromatic solvents, sparking interest in artificial receptors for fullerene recognition and separation. Traditional macrocyclic hosts such as γ-cyclodextrin, calix[n]arenes, and pillar[10]arene show similar binding affinities for fullerenes, but selective recognition of C60 and C70 remains challenging due to their similar diameters but different lengths. To address this, aromatic electron-rich units like anthracene have been used to create fullerene-selective receptors. Anthracene-based nanotubes, formed by anthracene-dimer building blocks and 2,6-dibromo-4-methoxypyridine rings, offer a promising solution. The hexagonal nanotube structure, with a cavity matching the size of C70 (∼1.2 nm diameter, ∼0.9 nm depth), selectively encapsulates C70 over C60 due to enhanced p–p interactions. This nanotube exhibits high binding selectivity (Ks ∼140 times) and pH stimuli-responsive properties, making it effective for enriching C70 from fullerene mixtures [42–51].

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs), especially SWCNTs, are known for their unique properties, such as high surface area, electrical conductivity, and mechanical strength, making them ideal candidates for gas sensing applications. Doping CNTs with metals such as Fe, Ni, and Pd enhances their gas sensing performance by creating active sites that facilitate the adsorption of target gases [52–54]. Previous research has shown that the response of CNTs can be significantly improved through modifications, such as filling multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) with vanadium oxide, which increases sensitivity to methane [55]. Additionally, Pd-decorated SWCNTs have demonstrated a capacity for remote sensing of CH4 concentrations, further showcasing the potential of modified CNTs in detecting gases [56]. In previous study, semiconducting (8,0) zigzag SWCNTs, chosen for their superior sensitivity to methane compared to other configurations, such as armchair nanotubes [57–59]. Larger diameter SWCNTs are particularly advantageous as they offer enhanced stability and the ability to incorporate metals for improved gas adsorption properties [60]. The adsorption behavior of these nanotubes with Fe, Ni, and Pd, examining how metal doping affects electronic and magnetic properties in the presence of methane, ultimately providing key insights for designing efficient methane detection devices[61,62]. This study investigates the structural, electronic, and optical properties of metal-doped anthracene nanotubes (An-NT), specifically focusing on variants A2-NT, A3-NT, A4-NT, A5-NT, A6-NT, and A7-NT. By employing computational methods, we explore the effects of different metal dopants on the stability and electronic properties of these nanotubes, alongside their optical characteristics and density of states. The findings highlight the significant enhancements in structural stability and electronic properties, demonstrating the potential of metal-doped anthracene nanotubes for applications in gas sensing and other advanced materials technologies.

In this study, we employed density functional theory (DFT) [63] simulations using Gaussian 16 [64] to investigate the electronic and optical properties of A2-NT, A3-NT, A4-NT, A5-NT, and A6-NT based materials, as well as the absorption changes that occur upon the adsorption of methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2) onto A6-NT. Our primary focus was on optimizing the structures and evaluating the electronic characteristics of these materials, with potential applications in gas sensing [65]. To achieve accurate results, we used the hybrid B3LYP functional [66,67], which has been extensively validated for studying the optimized geometries and electronic properties of carbon-based nanostructures [68–71]. The LANL2DZ basis set [72] was selected for its balance between computational efficiency and accuracy [73,74]. To properly account for Van der Waals interactions, which are essential for accurately modeling the interactions between A6-NT, its derivatives, and adsorbed gases like CH4 and CO2, we applied Grimme's dispersion correction (GD3) to the B3LYP functional [75]. For a deeper understanding of the electronic structure, we utilized Multiwfn software to calculate the partial density of states (PDOS) [76], allowing us to explore the electronic behavior of the materials in detail. Additionally, we analyzed the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO), Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO), and Mulliken charge distributions [77], which provided valuable insights into the electronic states and interactions within the nanotubes. These analyses helped to enhance our understanding of the materials' properties and their potential for sensing applications. Optical properties were assessed through time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) calculations, which were performed on the first fifty excited states to capture the absorption characteristics of the studied nanotubes. This comprehensive approach allowed us to thoroughly evaluate the electronic and optical behavior of these materials and their response to gas adsorption.
Here, we investigate the optimized structures, stability, electronic properties, and molecular electrostatic potential of undoped small anthracene nanotubes and their related materials.

Fig. 1 (a–f) illustrates the optimized structures of small anthracene nanotubes (An-NT), specifically A2-NT, A3-NT, A4-NT, A5-NT, A6-NT, and A7-NT where An is the number of anthracene units used to built the nanotube. These nanotubes are formed by connecting individual anthracene units through two carbon (2C) bridges between them, forming a cylindrical structure. The primary distinction between these nanotubes is the number of anthracene units (n), ranging from 2 to 7 for A2-NT through A7-NT. Despite the varying number of anthracene units, all nanotubes maintain a constant length of 9.326 Å, as shown in Fig. 1d. However, the nanotube diameters increase with the number of anthracene units. The diameters of A2-NT, A3-NT, A4-NT, A5-NT, A6-NT, and A7-NT increase progressively as the number of anthracene units rises. The respective diameters are 4.218 Å for A2-NT, 6.449 Å for A3-NT, 8.928 Å for A4-NT, 11.00 Å for A5-NT, 13.320 Å for A6-NT, and 15.435 Å for A7-NT, as shown in Fig. 1(a–f). This trend highlights the expansion in nanotube size as additional anthracene units are incorporated.

Table 1 presents structural parameters, including bond lengths (C1-C2, C2-C3, and C4-C6) and bond and dihedral angles (C1-C2-C3, C1-C2-C3-C8, and C2-C3-C8-C9) for all nanotubes. The C1-C2 and C2-C3 bond lengths remain consistent across the nanotubes. For example, the C1-C2 bond lengths range from 1.441 Å in A2-NT to 1.454 Å in A6-NT, while C2-C3 bond lengths range from 1.393 Å in A3-NT to 1.442 Å in A4-NT. These slight variations suggest minimal structural changes as the number of anthracene units increases. The C4-C6 bond length decreases slightly from 1.376 Å in A4-NT to 1.372 Å in A6-NT, indicating a tightening in this part of the structure as more anthracene units are added. The bond angle at C1-C2-C3 gradually increases from 116.326° in A2-NT to 118.058° in A5-NT, reflecting a slight expansion of the ring structure. The dihedral angles, particularly C1-C2-C3-C8 and C2-C3-C8-C9, increase significantly with the size of the nanotubes. The C1-C2-C3-C8 angle rises from 163.669° in A2-NT to 174.016° in A6-NT, while the C2-C3-C8-C9 angle increases from 149.015° in A4-NT to 159.367° in A6-NT, indicating a trend toward greater planarity. As the number of anthracene groups increases, the conjugation within the structure is enhanced due to the extended π-system, resulting in greater electronic delocalization. This increased conjugation contributes to the nanotubes' stability and planarity, with larger nanotubes like A6-NT and A7-NT showing superior electronic and optical properties. Even smaller nanotubes like A2-NT and A3-NT follow similar trends, though their reduced diameters and conjugation make them less electronically and structurally extensive compared to A7-NT.

The structure stability of A2-NT, A3-NT, A4-NT, A5-NT,A6-NT, and A7-NT was investigated using the binding energy (BE) [78], calculated using the formula:
(1)

E C

N H

E t
)
N t
where EC, EH, and Et represent the energy of carbon atoms, hydrogen atoms, and the total energy of the nanotube, respectively. NC, NH, and Nt denote the number of carbon atoms, hydrogen atoms, and the total number of atoms in the structure, respectively. The calculated BE (Binding Energy) values, as presented in Table 1, show an increasing trend as the number of anthracene units in the nanotubes increases. For A2-NT, A3-NT, A4-NT, A5-NT, A6-NT, and A7-NT, the BE values are −7.403 eV, −7.575 eV, −7.583 eV, −7.615 eV, −7.630 eV, and −7.640 eV, respectively. These values indicate that the stability of the anthracene nanotubes increases with the number of anthracene units. Specifically, A7-NT, with the highest BE of −7.640 eV, is the most stable nanotube, while A2-NT, with a BE of −7.403 eV, is the least stable. The increasing stability from A2-NT to A7-NT can be attributed to enhanced conjugation as the number of anthracene units increases.

As more anthracene groups are incorporated, the extent of π-conjugation across the nanotube structure increases, allowing for greater electronic delocalization. This delocalization helps distribute electronic energy more evenly across the structure, reducing the overall energy and leading to higher binding energies. For instance, the increase in BE from −7.403 eV in A2-NT to −7.640 eV in A7-NT is directly related to the additional anthracene units that enable more extensive π-conjugation. The greater stability observed in nanotubes like A6-NT (7.630 eV) and A7-NT (7.640 eV) compared to A4-NT (−7.583 eV) and A5-NT (−7.615 eV) highlights the importance of π-conjugation in enhancing the electronic stability of the nanotubes. The increased delocalization in larger nanotubes leads to higher binding energies, making them more stable. Thus, increasing the number of anthracene groups results in greater conjugation, improved electronic stability, and enhanced binding energy, as reflected in the BE values from A2-NT to A7-NT.The BE values of A2-NT to A7-NT range from −7.403 eV to −7.640 eV, with A7-NT being the most stable. In comparison, SWNT-C and DWNT-C have BE values of 7.124 eV and 7.220 eV, respectively [79]. A7-NT's BE of −7.640 eV is higher than both SWNT-C and DWNT-C, indicating greater stability due to enhanced π-conjugation with increasing anthracene units. As the number of anthracene groups increases, the stability and binding energy of the nanotubes improve, surpassing that of SWNT-C and DWNT-C. This suggests that anthracene-based nanotubes offer superior electronic stability.

The Partial Density of States (PDOS), the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO), the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO), and the energy gap (Eg) for A2-NT, A3-NT, A4-NT, A5-NT, A6-NT, and A7-NT in the gaseous phase were calculated. However, for clarity and to avoid redundancy, we present the data for A4-NT, A5-NT, and A6-NT as representative examples. Fig. 2 (a–c) displays the representative PDOS and HOMO/LUMO for A4-NT, A5-NT, and A6-NT. In these figures, the Fermi level is indicated by a dotted vertical line, with the HOMO on the left and the LUMO on the right. The results show significant changes in the PDOS, particularly for carbon (C) and hydrogen (H) atoms, as the number of anthracene groups increases from A4-NT to A6-NT. For instance, the HOMO PDOS peak for C rises from 1.28 eV for A4-NT to 1.81 eV for A6-NT, and the LUMO PDOS peak increases from 0.83 eV for A4-NT to 1.28 eV for A6-NT. This increasing density of states near the Fermi level reflects a higher electronic density as more anthracene units are incorporated into the nanotube. The delocalization of the HOMO and LUMO orbitals across the entire nanotube structure further emphasizes the high degree of conjugation in these systems, which contributes to the stability and electronic properties of the nanotubes.

The calculated Eg for A2-NT, A3-NT, A4-NT, A5-NT, A6-NT, and A7-NT provide further insight into the electronic properties of the NTs. The Eg values for A3-NT, A5-NT, and A7-NT are 1.853 eV, 1.980 eV and 1.731 eV, respectively, while the Eg values for A2-NT, A4-NT, and A6-NT are 1.468 eV, 1.610 eV, and 1.480 eV, respectively. These energy gaps are plotted in Fig. 3 , where an interesting pattern emerges: nanotubes with an even number of anthracene units (A2-NT, A4-NT, and A6-NT) exhibit lower Eg values compared to nanotubes with an odd number of anthracene units (A3-NT, A5-NT, and A7-NT). The plot of Eg forms a zigzag pattern, as shown in Fig. 3, with the energy gap alternating between higher values for odd-numbered nanotubes and lower values for even-numbered ones.

This zigzag behavior reflects alternating electronic properties between even- and odd-numbered anthracene nanotubes. The lower Eg values in the even-numbered systems can be attributed to greater π-conjugation and electronic delocalization, while the higher Eg in the odd-numbered nanotubes suggests reduced conjugation or different orbital interactions. These alternating trends underscore how small changes in the number of anthracene units significantly influence the nanotubes' electronic behavior.

The electrostatic potential (ESP) map [80] provides a visual representation of the electron density distribution within A2-NT, A3-NT, A4-NT, A5-NT, A6-NT, and A7-NT, offering valuable insights into their electronic properties. The ESP maps highlight regions of positive and negative electrostatic potentials, corresponding to specific areas within the nanotube structures. In these maps, regions with positive electrostatic potential are depicted in blue, typically corresponding to hydrogen atoms in C–H bonds. Areas with negative electrostatic potential are represented in red and yellow, often found around electronegative atoms or electron-rich regions, such as the bridge C–C bonds.

Fig. 4 presents the ESP maps for A4-NT, A5-NT, and A6-NT as representative examples. The maps for these nanotubes reveal distinct electrostatic regions, with positive potentials associated with hydrogen atoms and negative potentials associated with carbon–carbon bridge bonds. By examining the ESP maps of A2-NT, A3-NT, A4-NT, A5-NT, A6-NT, and A7-NT, we can observe how the distribution of electrostatic potentials evolves with the addition of more anthracene units. This detailed visualization helps to understand the variations in electronic properties across different nanotubes and provides insights into their stability and reactivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Doped anthracene nanotubes with transition and alkali metals
ce:section.ce:para: Since A6-NT and A7-NT have the highest binding energies (BE) and are more stable compared to A2-5-NT, we selected A6-NT to explore the effects of doping. Transition metals (Cu, Fe, Ni) and alkali metals (Li, Mg) were chosen to study their influence on the structural parameters, stability, electronic properties, and electrostatic potential (ESP).

The optimized structures of A6-NT after doping with transition and alkali metals are illustrated in Fig. 5 (b–f). Fig. 5a shows the optimized structure of 6A-NT, where two carbon atoms in each bridge A are replaced by transition metals such as Cu, Ni, and Fe, as well as alkali metals like Li and Mg. The selection of Cu, Fe, Ni, Li, and Mg for doping in this study was driven by their known catalytic properties and importance in material science. Transition metals such as Cu, Fe, and Ni are widely used to modify the electronic and catalytic properties of materials, enhancing gas adsorption, conductivity, and reactivity [81]. Alkali metals, including Li and Mg, are often employed to influence the charge distribution and electronic properties of materials [82]. These metal types were chosen to provide a comprehensive understanding of the doping effects on anthracene nanotubes, offering insights into their potential for improved gas sensing and catalytic applications. Since 6A-NT contains six bridge A-units, a total of six atoms of either transition or alkali metals are substituted into the structure. The replacement of carbon atoms with metal dopants significantly alters the structural and electronic properties of 6A-NT, potentially enhancing its chemical reactivity and stability. The choice of transition metals (Cu, Ni, and Fe) and alkali metals (Li and Mg) as dopants is particularly important due to their distinct electronic configurations. Transition metals, with their partially filled d-orbitals, can introduce new energy states and alter the electronic structure of 6A-NT, leading to potential changes in conductivity and catalytic behavior. In contrast, alkali metals like Li and Mg, with their s-orbital electrons, can influence the overall charge distribution and bonding characteristics within the nanostructure.

The structural effects of metal doping are substantial, as it can result in variations in bond lengths, bond angles, and dihedral angles around the doping sites, which directly impact the overall stability of the system. Therefore, it is crucial to thoroughly investigate how doping influences these structural parameters. To provide a comprehensive analysis, the effects of metal doping on bond lengths, bond angles, and dihedral angles should be systematically studied and summarized in Table 2 . This will allow for a clear comparison of the structural modifications introduced by different metal dopants.

Table 2 provides a detailed comparison of key structural parameters, such as bond lengths (C1-C2, C2-C3, C4-X), bond angles (C1-C2-C3), and dihedral angles (C1-C2-C3-C8, C2-C3-C8-C9) for A6-NT and its doped variants (A6-NT-6Cu, A6-NT-6Fe, A6-NT-6Ni, A6-NT-6Li, and A6-NT-6 Mg). The results highlight how metal doping with transition metals (Cu, Fe, Ni) and alkali metals (Li, Mg) affects the structural framework of A6-NT. The bond lengths in the C1-C2 and C2-C3 regions remain relatively stable across all doped structures, with variations within 0.02 Å, indicating that metal doping has a minor influence on the core C–C bonds of the A6-NT structure. However, significant changes are observed in the C4-X bond, where “X” represents the doping metal atom. In the pristine A6-NT, the C4-X bond length is 1.372 Å. After doping, where two carbon atoms of bridge A are replaced by the doping atom, the C4-X bond length increases significantly, ranging from 1.952 Å for Cu to 2.092 Å for Mg. Transition metals (Cu, Fe, Ni) exhibit shorter C4-X bonds compared to alkali metals (Li, Mg), which reflects the stronger interaction between transition metals and the carbon framework due to their higher electronegativity and d-orbital participation.

Doping also induces noticeable changes in bond angles. In pristine A6-NT, the C1-C2-C3 bond angle is 118.019°, but it increases after doping. The largest increase is observed for Fe doping (122.369°), followed closely by Li (122.102°), indicating that these elements have a more significant impact on the geometry around the dopant sites due to their specific bonding characteristics with the surrounding carbon atoms. The dihedral angles also change upon doping. In pristine A6-NT, the C1-C2-C3-C8 and C2-C3-C8-C9 dihedral angles are 174.016° and 159.367°, respectively. Upon doping, changes in these angles are observed, with the largest deviations found in the transition metal-doped structures. For example, Ni-doping leads to a C1-C2-C3-C8 dihedral angle of 163.639°, indicating a distortion in the planarity of the structure. Alkali metal doping, particularly with Li and Mg, results in dihedral angles closer to the original values, indicating that alkali metals induce less distortion in the structure compared to transition metals.

The BE values listed in Table 2 provide insights into the stability of A6-NT and its doped variants. The pristine A6-NT structure has the highest BE at 7.63 eV, indicating a highly stable configuration. When doping with transition and alkali metals, the BEs decrease, reflecting changes in stability due to the interaction of the doping atoms with the carbon framework. For the transition metal-doped structures, the BEs are slightly lower than that of pristine A6-NT, but they remain relatively high, suggesting that the system retains significantly stable. Specifically, A6-NT-6Cu shows a BE of −7.327 eV, A6-NT-6Fe has a BE of −7.468 eV, and A6-NT-6Ni has a BE of −7.444 eV. Among the transition metals, Fe-doping provides the highest BE, indicating that the interaction between Fe and the carbon network leads to a more stable configuration compared to Cu and Ni doping. For the alkali metal-doped structures, the BEs are lower than those of the transition metal-doped variants. A6-NT-6Li has a BE of −7.255 eV, while A6-NT-6 Mg has a BE of −7.265 eV. These values suggest that alkali metal doping leads to a slightly less stable structure compared to transition metal doping, likely due to the weaker interaction between alkali metals and the carbon framework. In summary, metal doping reduces the overall BE of A6-NT, with transition metal dopants (Fe, Ni, Cu) providing better stability than alkali metals (Li, Mg). The slight reduction in BE upon doping still maintains the system's overall stability, making it a promising candidate for various applications depending on the desired balance between stability and reactivity.

Fig. 6 (a–e) shows the HOMO and LUMO diagrams, along with the PDOS, for the doped A6-NT systems: A6-NT-6Cu, A6-NT-6Fe, A6-NT-6Ni, A6-NT-6 Mg, and A6-NT-6Li. The vertical dotted line represents the Fermi level, with the HOMO on the left side and the LUMO on the right. Additionally, Fig. 6(f) presents the Eg plot for all doped systems, highlighting the variations in band gaps caused by different dopants. It is evident from the diagrams and Eg plot that Cu and Li dopants effectively narrow the energy gap, while Fe, Ni, and Mg dopants result in wider energy gaps compared to the undoped A6-NT.

The narrowing of the Eg in A6-NT-6Cu (0.513 eV) can be attributed to Cu’s strong interaction with the electronic states near the Fermi level. The Cu dopant’s d-orbitals actively contribute to both the HOMO and LUMO, significantly modifying the electronic structure of A6-NT and enhancing charge transfer between these orbitals. This interaction reduces the gap between the occupied and unoccupied states, making the material more conductive. Similarly, the A6-NT-6Li system exhibits a reduced energy gap (0.601 eV), though Li does not directly participate in the HOMO or LUMO. The narrowing of the band gap in this case might be a result of indirect effects, such as charge redistribution caused by the introduction of Li, which influences the overall electronic structure. Compared to the Eg of the undoped A6-NT (1.480 eV), both Cu and Li significantly reduce the gap, enhancing the potential for electronic applications. In contrast, doping with Fe, Ni, and Mg leads to a wider energy gap compared to the undoped A6-NT. Although Fe partially participates in both the HOMO and LUMO, its orbital interactions are not as effective in reducing the gap. Fe’s d-orbitals interact differently with the A6-NT framework, leading to an increase in the energy gap rather than a reduction. For Ni and Mg, the lack of significant participation in the frontier orbitals results in even larger energy gaps. Ni’s d-orbitals and Mg’s s-orbitals do not contribute meaningfully to the electronic states near the Fermi level, preserving or even widening the gap between the HOMO and LUMO.

Fig. 6(f) illustrates these Eg trends, highlighting that Cu and Li dopants are effective in narrowing the band gap, while Fe, Ni, and Mg result in wider gaps. The widening of the gap with Fe, Ni, and Mg dopants can be explained by their weaker interaction with the electronic structure of A6-NT, which prevents them from contributing significantly to electronic transitions between the HOMO and LUMO. In summary, Cu and Li dopants play a crucial role in reducing the energy gap in A6-NT, making them ideal candidates for enhancing conductivity and optimizing electronic properties. Their interactions with the electronic states near the Fermi level—either directly (in the case of Cu) or indirectly (in the case of Li)—lead to a narrowed band gap, which is desirable for applications requiring improved electronic performance. In contrast, Fe, Ni, and Mg widen the Eg, limiting their effectiveness in applications that demand narrow-band-gap materials. This widening is primarily due to their limited participation in the HOMO and LUMO, resulting in weaker orbital interactions and a reduced ability to alter the electronic structure of A6-NT.

The optimized structures of A6-NT and its modified counterparts (A6-NT-6Cu, A6-NT-6Fe, A6-NT-6Ni, A6-NT-6 Mg, and A6-NT-6Li) after the adsorption of methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2) gases, as illustrated in Fig. 7 (a–l), highlight the influence of various metal dopants (Cu, Fe, Ni, Mg, and Li) on the adsorption behavior of the nanotube. The adsorption took place at the bridge A position, where the metal doping was introduced. The structural changes induced by gas adsorption were also examined. Table 3 presents key data, including the adsorption energy (Ea), bond distances before (db) and after (da) adsorption, and Mulliken charges, which help quantify the interactions between the gases and the nanotube. Negative Ea values signify an exothermic and favorable adsorption process. The pristine A6-NT exhibited lower adsorption energies for CH4 (−0.139 eV) and CO2 (−0.255 eV), indicating weaker interactions compared to the metal-doped nanotubes. Among the modified systems, A6-NT-6Fe demonstrated the highest adsorption energy for both CO2 (−2.077 eV) and CH4 (−1.599 eV), pointing to a particularly strong interaction. Meanwhile, A6-NT-6Li displayed the strongest interaction with CO2 (−1.413 eV).

In the case of CH4 adsorption, all systems indicated physisorption, with adsorption energies below −0.5 eV, except for A6-NT-6Fe, which showed chemisorption. For CO2 adsorption, most systems exhibited physisorption. However, A6-NT-6 Mg-CO2 (−0.512 eV) approached the threshold between physisorption and chemisorption, while A6-NT-6Li-CO2 and A6-NT-6Fe-CO2 clearly showed chemisorption, indicating a stronger bonding interaction. The adsorption characteristics observed for CH4 and CO2 on both pristine and metal-doped A6-NT nanotubes in this study are consistent with previous research findings. As noted in [83], the adsorption energies of CH4 (−0.139 eV) and CO2 (−0.255 eV) on the unmodified A6-NT align with earlier studies on carbon nanotubes, where weak physisorption typically results in adsorption energies within the range of −0.1 eV to −0.3 eV.

The bond distances between the adsorbate and the nanotube change after adsorption. For CO2, the bond distances generally decrease, indicating stronger binding. The most significant decrease is observed with A6-NT-6Li, where the distance reduces from 3.000 Å to 1.737 Å, which corresponds to its high adsorption energy. In the case of CH4, bond distances either increase or decrease depending on the metal dopant, with A6-NT-6Fe showing a substantial reduction from 3.000 Å to 2.159 Å, reflecting its strong interaction with CH4. The Mulliken charge values for the carbon atoms in CH4 and CO2 before adsorption are −0.578 and 0.590, respectively, and these values shift after adsorption. For CH4, the charge becomes more negative across all systems, with A6-NT-6 Mg (−0.690) and A6-NT-6Fe (−0.668) exhibiting the largest charge transfer, suggesting strong electron donation to CH4. For CO2, the charge becomes more positive after adsorption, indicating electron acceptance from the nanotube. The largest charge transfer occurs with A6-NT-6 Mg (0.683) and A6-NT-6Fe (0.673), showing strong interactions with CO2.

In summary, CH4 adsorption in all systems is dominated by physisorption, while CO2 adsorption is generally physisorption, except for A6-NT-6Li, where chemisorption occurs due to the significantly stronger interaction. Metal doping enhances the adsorption capabilities of the nanotube, particularly with Fe, Mg, and Li, showing substantial improvements. The Mulliken charge analysis and changes in bond distances also align with these adsorption trends.

We utilized the following equation
τ =
υ

e

a .
/
k B
T
to determine the recovery time, where υ−1 is the attempt frequency, Ea is the adsorption energy, kB is Boltzmann's constant and T is the temperature. The try frequency value of 10−12 s−1 was chosen [84]. From Table 3, the Ea and recovery time (τ) provide key insights into the interaction strength and From Table 3, the τ highlights the desorption dynamics after gas adsorption. For CH4 adsorption, rapid desorption is evident across all systems, with recovery times ranging from 2.953 × 1012 s (A6-NT-CH4) to 8.921 × 1012 s (A6-NT-6Fe-CH4). These fast times align with the relatively weak adsorption energies between −0.139 eV and −0.281 eV, indicative of physisorption, where weak van der Waals forces allow quick gas release. For CO2 adsorption, most systems also show fast recovery, such as A6-NT-CO2 (7.286 × 10−12 s) and A6-NT-6Cu-CO2 (1.007 × 10−11 s), supporting physisorption with adsorption energies below −0.5 eV. However, A6-NT-6Li-CO2 exhibits a significantly longer recovery time (6.015 × 10−8 s), driven by its much higher Ea (−1.413 eV), indicating chemisorption. Chemisorption involves stronger chemical bonds, which impede rapid desorption. Similarly, A6-NT-6 Mg-CO2, with an Ea of −0.512 eV, shows a longer recovery time of 5.378 × 10−11 s, reflecting stronger interactions that approach the chemisorption threshold. The picosecond-range recovery times observed in this study match prior findings [85], indicating rapid desorption kinetics. In summary, higher adsorption energies, such as those in A6-NT-6Li-CO2 and A6-NT-6 Mg-CO2, correlate with longer recovery times due to stronger binding, indicative of chemisorption. Systems with lower energies and faster recovery times, on the other hand, demonstrate physisorption.

The distribution of the HOMO and LUMO after the adsorption of CH4 and CO2 is depicted in Fig. 8 (a–f) and Fig. S1(a-f). These figures also provide PDOS for the systems A6-NT, A6-NT-6Cu, A6-NT-6Fe, A6-NT-6Ni, A6-NT-6Li, and A6-NT-6 Mg. The changes in the electronic structure due to gas adsorption are significant when compared to the pristine systems shown in Fig. 6(a–e). Following the adsorption of CH4 and CO2, shifts in the HOMO and LUMO levels are evident, indicating that the interaction between the gases and the substrate materials induces changes in the energy levels. However, the adsorbates do not directly contribute to the HOMO or LUMO states. Instead, they cause a redistribution of the electronic density within the adsorbent material (A6-NT and its metal-modified forms), which is reflected in the PDOS. The interaction with CH4 and CO2 influences the electronic environment around the Fermi level, leading to changes in the reactivity and conductivity of the material.

The redistribution of the HOMO and LUMO levels in the presence of adsorbed gases indicates an indirect influence on the substrate’s electronic properties. The PDOS confirms that although CH4 and CO2 alter the electronic states around the HOMO and LUMO, they do not integrate into these orbitals themselves. This behavior is consistent across all the modified systems, as seen in the PDOS for A6-NT-6Cu, A6-NT-6Fe, A6-NT-6Ni, A6-NT-6Li, and A6-NT-6 Mg. The adsorption of these gases shifts the electronic structure, causing the PDOS to change, which may affect the material’s catalytic or adsorption capabilities. Thus, the adsorption of CH4 and CO2 leads to significant modifications in the electronic structure of A6-NT and its metal-modified variants, as seen in the shifts in HOMO/LUMO levels and PDOS, while the gases themselves do not contribute directly to these orbitals. These observations can have important implications for understanding the reactivity and electronic behavior of such systems after gas adsorption.

The impact of gas adsorption on the Eg is highlighted in Fig. 9 , which displays the Eg values for A6-NT both before and after the adsorption of CH4 and CO2. The red column represents the pristine A6-NT and its modified NT before gas adsorption, the green column represents Eg values following CH4 adsorption and the blue column shows Eg values after CO2 adsorption. The specific Eg values for A6-NT and its doped derivatives after the adsorption of CH4 and CO2 are summarized in Table 4 . As depicted in Fig. 9, CH4 adsorption does not significantly affect the energy gaps in most systems. For example, A6-NT-6Cu-CH4 shows a slight reduction in Eg to 0.439 eV, while A6-NT-6Fe-CH4 and A6-NT-6Ni-CH4 exhibit only minimal changes in their energy gaps. This indicates that the interaction between CH4 and the metal-doped nanotubes is relatively weak and does not substantially alter their electronic properties. Similarly, the adsorption of CO2 results in only minor variations in the energy gaps. For A6-NT-6Cu-CO2, the Eg remains nearly unchanged, decreasing slightly to 0.503 eV. A6-NT-6Li-CO2 also exhibits a small reduction, with the energy gap narrowing to 0.564 eV. Other systems, such as A6-NT-6Fe-CO2 and A6-NT-6Ni-CO2, show comparable slight decreases in Eg, reinforcing the observation that CO2 adsorption has a limited impact on the electronic properties of the nanotubes.

Fig. 10 shows the calculated absorption spectra for A2-NT, A3-NT, A4-NT, A5-NT, and A6-NT based materials, as well as the absorption changes after the adsorption of methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2) onto A6-NT. The absorption shifts observed in the figure are a result of changes in electronic transitions (ET) between the HOMO and LUMO, as indicated in Table 5 . These shifts provide insight into how the electronic structure of the nanotube materials responds to molecular interactions, specifically through the involvement of different orbitals. Table 5 provides critical details of the excited states for each compound, such as the maximum absorption wavelength (λmax), transition energy (TE), electronic transition (ET), oscillator strength (f), and transition coefficient (TC). The ET describe which orbitals are involved in these excitations. For example, A2-NT, with a λmax of 351 nm, shows an electronic transition from H-6 to L + 2, which requires a transition energy of 3.53 eV. This corresponds to absorption in the UV region. In contrast, A6-NT has a λmax of 646 nm, with a transition from H-3 to L, reflecting a much lower transition energy of 1.92 eV, allowing it to absorb in the visible to near-infrared region.

Adsorption of gases like CH4 and CO2 onto A6-NT leads to a shift in the ETs and thus the absorption spectra. For instance, A6-NT-CH4 exhibits a shift to λmax = 637 nm (TE of 1.94 eV) due to the transition from H to L + 3, while A6-NT-CO2 shifts to λmax = 647 nm with a slightly lower TE of 1.91 eV (H-2 to L transition). These small shifts in λmax indicate that the gas molecules interact with the electronic structure of A6-NT, altering the energy levels and affecting the overall absorption. Doping A6-NT with transition metals such as Cu, Fe, Ni, Li, and Mg produces even more significant changes in the absorption spectra. For example, A6-NT-6Cu-CH4 shifts the λmax dramatically to 1445 nm, corresponding to a transition energy of 0.86 eV (H-8 to L + 2 transition). This large redshift indicates a stronger interaction between the nanotube and the adsorbate, resulting in a lower transition energy and absorption in the near-infrared region. Similar effects are observed for other dopants: A6-NT-6Fe-CH4 exhibits λmax at 689 nm (H-1 to L + 4 transition), while A6-NT-6Ni-CH4 shifts to λmax at 676 nm (H-5 to L transition). These shifts highlight the role of dopants in altering the orbital interactions and transition energies, leading to significant changes in optical properties. The f values for these transitions are also important in determining the intensity of absorption. A6-NT-6Ni and A6-NT-6 Mg, for instance, have higher oscillator strengths, reflecting stronger transitions and more intense absorption. Conversely, compounds like A6-NT-6Cu-CH4, with a lower oscillator strength, indicate weaker transitions and less intense absorption. In conclusion, the absorption shifts observed in Fig. 10 are strongly dependent on the electronic transitions between specific orbitals, as detailed in Table 5. The adsorption of gases like CH4 and CO2 causes subtle changes in the electronic structure, while doping with metals leads to more significant modifications. These shifts in absorption are driven by changes in the transition energy and the orbitals involved in the electronic transitions, making these nanotube-based materials promising candidates for applications such as gas sensing and catalysis, where adsorption-induced changes in optical properties are critical. A2-NT to A7-NT exhibit significantly higher λmax values compared to SWNT-C and DWNT-C [79], indicating extended absorption into the visible and near-infrared regions. SWNT-C has a λmax of 455.10 nm (TE = 2.1603 eV), while DWNT-C has a λmax of 574.28 nm (TE = 2.159 eV). In contrast, A6-NT has a λmax of 646 nm (TE = 1.92 eV), and A7-NT shifts further, showing lower transition energies. These results highlight that anthracene nanotubes, with their enhanced π-conjugation, absorb at longer wavelengths, offering a broader absorption range compared to SWNT-C and DWNT-C.

This study demonstrates the significant impact of metal doping on the structural, electronic, optical, and gas adsorption properties of anthracene nanotubes (An-NT). The optimized structures reveal that doping with transition metals (Cu, Ni, Fe) and alkali metals (Li, Mg) leads to notable alterations in bond lengths and stability, particularly in the A6-NT structure with Fe doping, which exhibits the highest stability. Electronic analysis shows that certain dopants, especially Cu and Li, effectively reduce the band gap, enhancing the conductivity of the nanotubes, while others like Ni and Mg result in increased band gaps, indicating diminished conductivity. Optical properties reveal significant changes in the absorption spectra linked to shifts in the density of states (DOS), providing avenues for potential applications in optoelectronic devices. Furthermore, the enhanced gas adsorption capabilities of Fe-doped A6-NT and A6-NT for CH4 and CO2 underline their suitability for gas sensing and storage applications. Overall, the findings highlight the versatility of metal-doped anthracene nanotubes in various technological applications, paving the way for future research and development in this field.
